coredata.dc:title: Dual emissive water-based flexo ink from tapioca-derived carbon dots for anti-counterfeiting applications
coredata.dc:creator: Ullal, Namratha
Lewis, Prinston Melroy
Sunil, Dhanya
Kulkarni, Suresh D.
P.J., Anand
K., Udaya Bhat
coredata.dc:description: Counterfeiting of high-value items is a challenging menace worldwide, and luminescent nanoparticles-based security inks have promising applications while addressing this global issue. As Carbon dots (CDs) show attractive functional properties, hydrophilic CDs were prepared through hydrothermal approach from tapioca starch as an eco-friendly precursor. CDs with bluish-green fluorescence emission under 365 nm UV light illumination was obtained using column chromatography technique. TCSPC studies indicated the presence of blue and green emitters with average lifetimes of 1.12 and 1.61 ns, respectively. The graphitic and polycrystalline nature of CDs (~9 nm) with negatively charged surface groups was confirmed through ATR-IR, XPS, RS, XRD, SAED, DLS and TEM. An eco-friendly water-based security ink for flexography printing was formulated, and the influence of the ink components on the fluorescence of CDs were studied. The prints on UV dull paper displayed good abrasion resistance, densitometry and colorimetric values. The letters written using the invisible ink exhibited good security features including excellent covertness under daylight, and a bluish-green emission under 365 nm UV light. Moreover, a yellow emission was perceived using a yellow-orange pass filter under the same light source. The surface morphology and electrical property investigations suggested the use of flexo-ink in flexible printed electronics for anti-counterfeiting. These results propose that Tapioca-derived CDs can enable unique optical features in its eco-friendly ink formulation to demonstrate promising security applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Eco-friendly tapioca precursor
Carbon dots
Colum chromatography
Fluorescence
Water-based flexographic security ink
Anti-counterfeiting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Separation and isolation of CDs by column chromatography
Printing using eco-friendly ink and study of flexo-print proof properties
Hydrothermal process for the synthesis of tapioca-derived CDs
Identification of functional groups and optical features of the separated fractions
Formulation of security ink using CDs for flexography printing
Analysis of flexo-coats/prints on UV dull paper substrates
Anti-counterfeiting application of flexo-ink formulation with dual emissive CDs
Dual emissive ink for document authentication
Flexo ink from CDs for flexible electronics in anti-counterfeiting

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of synthesis of CDs from Tapioca starch.
a) ATR-IR and b) absorbance spectra of unpurified CD sample and fractions1–5.
Fluorescence spectra of a) unpurified, b) first, c) second, d) third, e) fourth and f) fifth fractions.
X-ray photoelectron spectra of CDs in fraction 5 a) survey scan, and full resolution spectra of b) carbon, c) oxygen and d) nitrogen before Ar etching.
a) XRD spectra of CDs form fraction 5 and b) graphite-2H (00-041-1487). RS of CDs from fraction 5 at c) 785 nm and d) 532 nm excitation.

TEM images displaying a) carbon nanotubes and b) CDs. c) SAED pattern of CDs with different diffraction planes. d) EDS spectrum displaying elements carbon, oxygen, nitrogen and sulphur. Elemental mapping consisting of e) all elements f) carbon, g) nitrogen, h) oxygen and i) sulphur marked in different colour codes.
TCSPC measurement at a) λex = 370 nm and λem = 470 nm, b) λex = 370 nm and λem = 534 nm, c) λex = 470 nm and λem = 534 nm and d) λex = 500 nm and λem = 534 nm.
a) Absorption and b) emission spectra of CDs dispersed in different components of ink and the final ink formulation.

a) Graphical representation of L*, a* and b* values for print proofs subjected to a) different UV exposure cycles and b) rub test. c) Excitation and emission spectra of paper samples. Photographs of ‘MAHE’ cut out of UV dull paper coated with the CD- flexo ink when viewed under d) daylight, e) 365 nm UV light and f) yellow pass filter and 365 nm UV light. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
The components and composition of water-based flexo-ink using CDs.

head.ce:title: Dual emissive water-based flexo ink from tapioca-derived carbon dots for anti-counterfeiting applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Manipal Institute of Technology, Manipal Academy of Higher Education, Manipal 576104, Karnataka, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Manipal Institute of Technology, Manipal Academy of Higher Education, Manipal 576104, Karnataka, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre of Applied Nanosciences, Department of Atomic and Molecular Physics, Manipal Academy of Higher Education, Manipal 576104, Karnataka, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Atomic and Molecular Physics

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Centre of Applied Nanosciences, Department of Atomic and Molecular Physics, Manipal Academy of Higher Education, Manipal 576104, Karnataka, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Manipal Technologies Limited, Manipal 576104, Karnataka, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Manipal Technologies Limited, Manipal 576104, Karnataka, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Metallurgical and Materials Engineering, National Institute of Technology, Surathkal 575025, Karnataka, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Metallurgical and Materials Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Metallurgical and Materials Engineering, National Institute of Technology, Surathkal 575025, Karnataka, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Counterfeiting of high-value items is a challenging menace worldwide, and luminescent nanoparticles-based security inks have promising applications while addressing this global issue. As Carbon dots (CDs) show attractive functional properties, hydrophilic CDs were prepared through hydrothermal approach from tapioca starch as an eco-friendly precursor. CDs with bluish-green fluorescence emission under 365 nm UV light illumination was obtained using column chromatography technique. TCSPC studies indicated the presence of blue and green emitters with average lifetimes of 1.12 and 1.61 ns, respectively. The graphitic and polycrystalline nature of CDs (~9 nm) with negatively charged surface groups was confirmed through ATR-IR, XPS, RS, XRD, SAED, DLS and TEM. An eco-friendly water-based security ink for flexography printing was formulated, and the influence of the ink components on the fluorescence of CDs were studied. The prints on UV dull paper displayed good abrasion resistance, densitometry and colorimetric values. The letters written using the invisible ink exhibited good security features including excellent covertness under daylight, and a bluish-green emission under 365 nm UV light. Moreover, a yellow emission was perceived using a yellow-orange pass filter under the same light source. The surface morphology and electrical property investigations suggested the use of flexo-ink in flexible printed electronics for anti-counterfeiting. These results propose that Tapioca-derived CDs can enable unique optical features in its eco-friendly ink formulation to demonstrate promising security applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Forging or illegal reproduction of documents/products is a global menace that has serious impact on individuals, companies, and governments. Anti-counterfeit products in the market can help prevent faking of legal documents, currencies, pharmaceuticals, and agrochemical products [1–3]. Among the various strategies being developed and implemented, security printing using smart fluorescent materials holds great promise because it can provide concealed properties that are difficult to duplicate. Moreover, fast printing speed, high resolution, and assorted designs coupled with versatile applications are other attractive features. Different materials including metal nanoclusters, rare earth-doped nanostructures, inorganic perovskite nanocrystals, semiconducting nanocrystals, nanorods and organic molecules [4–12] have been used to formulate fluorescent security inks. In this context, carbon dots (CDs) have displayed unique features with immense potential for prospective application in security printing. They are discrete, polycrystalline (graphitic) carbon with typically <10 nm diameter. These carbonaceous materials with good fluorescence emission have proven increasingly popular in bioimaging, photo-electric applications and sensing of metal-ions as well as other chemicals due to their low toxicity [13].

In order to realize covert features in security printing by utilizing CDs as fluorophores, optimizing its distinctive structural, morphological, and optical properties by varying the carbon source, synthesis conditions, surface functionalization, pH, solvent, purification methods etc. are of vital importance. It is well-known that natural and sustainable carbon sources are highly advantageous to synthetic ones as the production cost and environmental pollution is less. CDs from natural precursors including; i) plant biomass such as chrysanthemum buds [14], willow leaves [15], Magnolia liliiflora flower [16], bougainvillea leaves [17], red cabbage [18], and pine pollen [19], ii) food materials such as gelatin [20] and egg white [13], and iii) waste materials such as dwarf banana peel [21], turtle shell [22], cigarette butts [23] and wasted coffee grounds [24] have been reported for anti-counterfeit applications. Except for the chrysanthemum buds-derived red-emitting CDs, the emissions from majority of these nanodots prepared for security applications were concentrated in the blue wavelength range, which limits their application. Moreover, purification, separation and isolation of pure CDs possessing optimized optical properties is crucial for structural analysis and desired applications. There are extensive reports on purification of CDs derived from natural precursors, especially through dialysis method [25]. However, the use of column chromatography as purification technique for CDs derived from natural precursors is yet to be explored. Besides, there is no detailed report on the formulation of an eco-friendly, water-based security ink from fluorescent CDs for cost-effective and high speed flexography printing as well as a comprehensive evaluation of the properties of the print proofs.

In the present study, we report the synthesis of CDs from Tapioca flesh as a starch-rich carbon precursor through mild hydrothermal treatment, and their further purification through column chromatography approach. The fractions obtained are analysed using Attenuated Total Reflection Infrared (ATR-IR), UV–Vis and photoluminescence spectroscopic techniques. Further, the CDs of interest are characterized using Raman Spectroscopy (RS), X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS), X-ray powder Diffraction (XRD), elemental mapping, Energy Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy (EDS), Selected Area Electron Diffraction (SAED), Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS), Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) and Time Correlated Single Photon Count (TCSPC) techniques. Furthermore, a water-based formulation using the CDs as fluorescent material for flexographic printing is formulated and printed on UV-dull paper substrate. The print properties including lightfastness, colorimetric and densitometric values, and abrasion resistance are investigated. The eco-friendly flexo-ink formulated from the dual (blue and green) emissive CDs can offer superior security features to the prints: i) invisible under daylight, ii) intense blue emission and iii) yellow emission while using a yellow-orange long pass filter upon 365 nm illumination. Moreover, the surface morphology and the electrical properties of the print proofs are analysed for plausible use of CD-derived flexo ink in flexible printed electronics. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report on the purification of CDs obtained from natural source using column chromatography, and subsequent formulation of an eco-friendly flexo-ink combined with a detailed study on the properties of the print proofs for security application.

In order to synthesize the CDs, Tapioca starch from the roots of Cassava plant, which is a starch rich staple food for people in tropical countries, was chosen as the naturally available carbon source. Besides starch, the precursor material contains proteins, fibres, fat and non-starch residues in trace amounts [26]. The inner flesh of the freshly purchased Tapioca root was meticulously sliced and separated from the surrounding fibrous tissue. Further, it was cleansed with distilled water, cut into pieces, and baked. The pieces were ground well, washed with deionized water to remove the contaminants, dried and powdered using pestle and mortar. About 200 mg of Tapioca powder was dissolved in 5 mL deionized water and transferred into a 25 mL Teflon coated autoclave. About 40 % NaOH (1.2 g dissolved in 3 mL) and 1 mL of acetone were added. The final volume of the mixture was made up to 10 mL with deionized water, and allowed to undergo hydrothermal reaction at 175 °C. Further, the sample was filtered to remove solid contaminants, and the filtrate was employed in the subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Separation and isolation of CDs by column chromatography
ce:section.ce:para: A column of 150 mm length and 35 mL maximum volume was purchased from ABG Initiative. The stationary phase silica gel of 400–700 Mesh was procured from SRL Chemicals. The solvent mixture chosen was dichloromethane (DCM) and methanol, both obtained from SRL Chemicals.

The ATR-IR spectra were taken on Shimadzu-IR Spirit analyzer using 40 scans with a resolution of 4 cm−1. The absorption spectra were obtained from 200 to 800 nm on Cary 60 UV–Vis Spectrophotometer. A 1.0 nm bandwidth and scan rate of 4800 nm/min was used for the analysis. The quantum yields and emission spectra of CDs in a quartz cuvette of 10 mm path length were acquired at room temperature using JASCO spectrofluorometer FP-8300. For analysis, excitation bandwidth of 5 nm and emission bandwidth of 2.5 nm were used. The excitation wavelength ranged from 370 to 390 nm and data was collected from 300 to 700 nm. XPS data was collected using Omicron XPS system (Nanotechnology Ltd., Germany). The excitation source used for detecting elements was Mg-Kα and Al-Kα (non-monochromatic). Analyses were carried out before and after Argon (Ar) etching for 10 min with spectral scan ranging from 63.6 eV to 1253.6 eV. The spectra were obtained both as survey scan and high resolution scan with data computed as Counts vs Binding energy (eV). The XRD spectrum was recorded on Malvern PANalytical, Empyrean 3rd Gen using Cu Kα as source with wavelength 1.5405 and 2θ range from 10 to 80 degrees at a scan rate of 37.45 times per step. The generator voltage was set to 45 V and tube current of 40 A with divergence slit maintained fixed. The Raman spectra was obtained from Renishaw Compact Raman Spectrometer with exposure time of 10 s at continuous scan. The source used was laser power of 0.05 % for 532 nm and 0.00001 % for 785 nm. The surface charge of CDs was recorded using Horiba Scientific Nano Particle analyzer SZ-100. The TEM images were recorded using JEM 2100 F model using a 160 kV electron source. About 10 μL of the sample was dispersed in 1 mL methanol and drop casted on 200 mesh carbon coated copper grid and dried under ambient condition. The images of CDs were analysed using Image-J software. SAED, TEM-EDS and elemental mapping were performed using Talos F200 S model. TCSPC was recorded using DeltaFlex TCSPC Lifetime Fluorimeter equipped with Mai Tai Laser (λex = 340–510 nm) as short-pulse oscillator and HPPD with conventional PMT design.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Printing using eco-friendly ink and study of flexo-print proof properties
ce:section.ce:para: The Digital GSM tester was used to measure the grammage (in gsm) of unprinted and printed paper substrate. RK Flexiproof 100 at 50 m/min, Anilox roll of 9.5 BCM, chambered doctor blade inking equipment, and photopolymer plate were used to print the paper samples. The density, L*, a*, and b* readings were recorded by placing the Xrite i1 Pro spectrophotometer on various paper samples. The Sutherland ink rub tester was used to test the abrasion resistance of the ink on a printed sample. Atomic force microscope (AFM) image was obtained using Flex-Axiom AFM instrument, M/s Nanosurf with scan resolution ranging from 100 to 10 μm. The data was analysed using Gwyddion-2.60 software to obtain 2D and 3D topological views of the sample. The electrical characteristics were measured at room temperature using the Keithley Hall Effect setup at 3706 A.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrothermal process for the synthesis of tapioca-derived CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Hydrothermal method was adopted for the synthesis of CDs as it offers benefits of being more economical, lesser-toxic, and environmentally friendly. Acetone and NaOH was added to Tapioca flour to improve the mobility of glucose molecules that forms the integral part of starch. The thermal treatment of the reaction mixture went through various phases including the initial gelatinization process, disintegration of amylopectin-amylose to form several polymeric or oligomeric compounds, glucose, and finally the CDs (Fig. 1 ) [27]. The formation of CDs was observed through the colour transformation from an initial white gelatinous to a final opaque pale-brown solution. The hydrothermal reaction was performed for different durations of 1, 3, 5 and 7 h to choose the optimal reaction time. Aliquots of CD samples obtained for each of the four mentioned reaction times were analysed using UV spectroscopy. The respective absorption spectra as presented in Supplementary Fig. 1 (SF1) exhibits two characteristic peaks at ~244 nm and ~320 nm for all samples representing transitions including π → π⁎ and n → π⁎. As no appreciable shift was observed in the absorption spectra, the reaction time for the convenient synthesis of CDs was optimized as 1 h.

The reaction products obtained after hydrothermal treatment generally contain mixtures of molecular intermediate and side products in addition to various CD fractions. Hence, purification and separation of different fractions of CDs and isolation of CDs possessing desired optical properties was achieved using column chromatography. Though few literature evidences suggested centrifugation and ultracentrifugation techniques [28], the CDs were not sufficiently purified. Several studies also reported the use of dialysis approach to purify the raw CD samples [29], but selecting the membrane with optimal molecular weight cut-off is very crucial because higher molecular weight contaminants may stay in the bag, while tiny CDs may flow through [30]. Despite the fact that polarity variations may successfully separate reactants, by-products, and products, simple column chromatographic separation remains underutilised, and there are no investigations on the purification of CDs obtained from natural biomass using this technique. Therefore, to investigate the different fractions obtained and to identify the CDs of interest with good optical properties for the desired anti-counterfeiting application, preparative column chromatography was performed in conjunction with DCM and methanol as a solvent system in the present study.

To choose a suitable solvent mixture for the fractionation process, thin layer chromatography was initially performed. Various ratios of DCM:methanol in sequence of increasing polarity; 100 % DCM, 90 % DCM:10 % methanol, 50 % DCM:50 % methanol and 30 % DCM:70 % methanol were chosen as better elution systems. Further, column chromatography was used for separation of different fractions, through band separation observed in the column, based on polarity difference. About 1 mL of the filtrate (unpurified sample) was used for the separation process. The SF2 presents the photographs of the separation in the column under the chosen solvent system along with the images of TLC separation. Five fractions of CDs were obtained after 6 h duration: fractions 1, 2, 3 and 4 were soluble in DCM and fraction 5 was soluble in methanol.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Identification of functional groups and optical features of the separated fractions
ce:section.ce:para: The intrinsic functional groups present in unpurified CDs sample and purified CD fractions were identified through ATR-IR analysis, and the respective plots are depicted in Fig. 2a. Unpurified fraction displayed numerous small peaks around 1186 cm−1 corresponding to CO stretch, medium intensity peak at 1638 cm−1, which might belong to carbonyl group of amide, less intense peak at 2882 cm−1 corresponding to CH stretch and broad peak at 3288 cm−1 belonging collectively to carboxylic (COOH) and amino (−NH2) groups. However, the different fractions collected after the separation displayed fewer peaks. The first fraction showed intense peak at 748 cm−1 that might correspond to the CCl stretch of dichloromethane solvent, while the peak at 1271 cm−1 may belong to CO stretch. The second and fourth fractions exhibited almost similar trend in the vibrational peaks, while additional weak peaks observed at 1638 cm−1 and 3281 cm−1 in the second fraction might be due to CO and COOH stretching. The third and fifth fractions displayed a very less intense peak at 1016 cm−1 corresponding to CO stretch, a peak of medium intensity at 1638 cm−1 due to CO stretch and a broad peak ranging from 3200 to 3500 cm−1 belonging to COOH stretch and –NH2.

Further, all the five fractions were subjected to absorption measurements and the respective spectra are portrayed in Fig. 2b. In the case of unpurified sample, two distinct peaks at 244 nm corresponding to π → π* and 330 nm belonging to n → π* transitions were observed. Though there was no appreciable difference in the spectra between unpurified and fraction 1 samples, an additional peak at 232 nm was observed in the latter [30]. A blue-shift was observed for fractions 2 and 3, wherein both the absorption peaks lie at the higher energy region. However, a red shift was observed in fractions 4 and 5 compared to fractions 2 and 3, but almost similar to fraction 1.

The photographs of unpurified sample and all the five fractions under daylight and 365 nm UV illumination are presented in SF3 and SF4, respectively. In previous reports, the emission spectra of the synthesized CDs were studied with multiple excitations along with their corresponding emission peaks, indicating the presence of other fluorophores [31]. However, in the present study, an attempt was made to investigate the individual emission properties of the resulting fractions after separation using column chromatography. The characteristic emission spectra of the unpurified and all five fractions are presented in Fig. 3a–f. As the emission spectra of unpurified sample recorded at 470 nm displayed two broad emission peaks at 490 nm and 550 nm, it can be inferred that majority of the fluorophores show emission in the visible region. In fraction 1, the emitters exposed to a 370 nm excitation source displayed emission at 411 nm and 435 nm, which lies in the UV region. Moreover, when the second, third, and fourth fractions were excited at 370 nm, they displayed three emission peaks at 410–411 nm, 435 nm, and 463 nm. The wavelength (435 nm) that corresponds to the violet zone was the most intense among all the three emissions. However, when excited at 370 nm, fraction 5 showed emissions at 470 nm and 534 nm. As opposed to other fractions, the emissions lie in the blue and green regions of the visible spectrum, which was of interest. A molecule's inherent optical efficiency is evaluated based on quantum yield measurement, and several reports projected quantum yield estimations using Quinine sulphate as reference by relative measurement [32]. However, in the present study absolute method was used to determine the quantum yield by measuring the amount of emitted photon with absorbed photons of that sample. Interestingly, the quantum yield measured for fraction 5 (λex = 370 nm) was 1.167 %, which was significantly higher than 0.156 % recorded for the unpurified sample of (λex = 470 nm).

As CDs in fraction 5 displayed fluorescence emission in the blue and green regions of the visible spectrum with prospective potential to be explored for anti-counterfeiting application, further studies were performed using this fraction. The CDs from fraction 5 were characterized using XPS, XRD, RS, DLS, TEM and TCSPC.

The results obtained from the ATR-IR spectrum analysis of fraction 5 was validated by examining the XPS data. The Fig. 4 and SF5 depict the XPS results before and after Ar etching, respectively. Peaks were observed at 284.2 eV for C1s, 532.3 eV for O1s, 400.3 eV for N1s and a less intense peak at 170 eV for S2p in the survey scan [33]. The original C1s spectra was fitted with three Gaussian peaks having maximum values of 285.4, 286.1, and 289.5 eV, corresponding to the CC (sp3 hybridisation), CO, and CO chemical states. The O1s spectra was deconvoluted to reveal CO and CO peaks at 531 and 533 eV, respectively. When compared to the Lorential curve produced in the case of carbon and oxygen, the peak corresponding to nitrogen at 400.3 eV appeared extremely small in the survey spectra, while the full resolution spectrum appeared as noise. This could imply that the existence of nitrogen was minimal, might be due to trace amounts of proteins in the precursor material. Nevertheless, deconvolution yielded two peaks at 399.9 and 401.2 eV, which can be attributed to the amino and pyrrolic groups, respectively. Besides, the presence of a small peak for elemental sulphur might be due to low suphur containing amino acid levels in Tapioca [34]. Ar ion etching was carried out for 10 min to check for the presence of any additional groups or any spectral shifts. There was marginal shift in peaks for C1s spectra, whereas no shift was observed for O1s when the top surface layer was removed. However, there was a minimum shift for N1s from 401.2 eV to 400.1 eV. The quantitative estimation of individual elements was not possible as the exact molecular composition and ratio of the components of the natural precursor material is unknown.

As reported in literature, XRD pattern for CDs are generally broad and are centred at 22° and 40°, which substantiates its partly amorphous nature [35]. On the contrary, slight shift in peaks were observed for earlier reports on CDs derived from Chia seeds indicating presence of partial graphitic carbon [36]. Therefore, XRD patterns of Tapioca-derived CDs of fraction 5 presented in Fig. 5a were examined and compared with that of graphite-2H (00-041-1487) displayed in Fig. 5b. The Fig. 5a demonstrated sharp diffraction peaks at 2θ = 25.5, 43.3 and 50.1 degrees and small diffraction peaks at 2θ = 44.1 and 52.1 degrees. On comparison with Fig. 5b, the intense peaks at 25.5, 43.3 and 50.1 degrees of Tapioca derived CDs fall in close proximity with (002), (100) and (102) planes of diffraction, while the smaller diffraction peaks at 44.1 and 52.1 degrees lie closer to (101) and (004) diffraction planes [37].

Previous literature also provides evidence for existence of two broad peaks in the RS positioned at 1360 cm−1 and 1592 cm−1, mainly for sp2 (distorted carbon) and sp3 carbon (graphitic carbon) pointing towards quasi-amorphous nature of CDs [38]. Therefore, RS of CDs as presented in Fig. 5c & d were collected to check for any changes when excited at two different wavelengths of 785 and 532 nm. Different peaks were visible upon using two different excitations (near Infrared at 785 nm and visible at 532 nm), which indicated the existence of several forms of carbon nanoparticles in fraction 5. An intense peak was observed at 120 cm−1 (λex = 785 nm) (Fig. 5c), which suggested radial breathing mode vibration found only in carbon nanotubes. Many peaks were observed upon excitation at 532 nm (Fig. 5d), which indicated a complex chemical composition. Breathing mode vibration for polyaromatic hydrocarbons and cyclic systems is generally indicated by medium intensity peaks at 979 cm−1 and 1168 cm−1 [39]. Two peaks at 1065 cm−1 and 2061 cm−1 suggested the existence of ether group. Graphitic carbon peaks were observed at 1275 cm−1 in the Ds band, with a significant shift in the distorted band (D band) at 1426 cm−1 [40]. Furthermore, a weaker G signal or graphitic peak was seen at 1570 cm−1. The most prominent peak was found at 1789 cm−1, which indicated high concentration of carbonyl (C=O) groups. Peaks at 1676 cm−1, 1921 cm−1, and 2203 cm−1 specified the presence of alkene (C=C), polycumulene (=C=C=), and polyyne (-CC-) due to the carbonaceous character of the compound [41].

In order to comprehend the surface charge and stability of the CDs, the zeta potential was measured by dispersing the nanoparticles in deionized water. A sharp peak centred at −29.5 mV as depicted in SF6 indicated the presence of numerous negatively charged groups on the surface of the CDs. They did not show any signs of precipitation or agglomeration even after storage for three months indicating colloidal stability due to mutual repulsion.

The TEM images of the CDs in fraction 5 showed the presence of both carbon nanotubes (Fig. 6a) and CDs (Fig. 6b). The average diameter of the dispersed CDs was estimated to be 9.54 nm. Though XPS and ATR-IR confirmed the presence of specific elements along with the nature of hybridisation and functional groups, the uniformity of groups was studied by performing elemental mapping, SAED and EDS. The SAED images of CDs as presented in Fig. 6c depicted ring diffraction in combination with bright spot patterns representing planes belonging to (002), (100) and (102), supporting the data obtained from the XRD study, which validated the polycrystalline nature of CDs. Furthermore, TEM-EDS analysis was carried out across a specified region as shown in Fig. 6d, which indicated the presence of abundant carbon along with lesser amounts of other constituents such as oxygen, nitrogen, and sulphur. As a quantitative assessment was not possible, elemental mapping was done over the same area, and the various elements are highlighted in different colours as shown in Fig. 6e–i. Carbon and nitrogen were found to be densely inhabited across a small region as evidenced by EDS measurements, although oxygen was abundant. The lesser amount of suphur was evidenced as sparse distribution in the elemental mapping.

Furthermore, as per previous literature, the fluorescence lifetime values for CDs derived from natural precursors range from 1 to 9 ns [42–45], whereas purified CDs displayed lifetime as high as 10 ns in methanol with single exponential photoluminescence decay [46]. Therefore, to measure the fluorescence decays of the tapioca-derived CDs of fraction 5 in time domain, TCSPC was measured with excitation at 370, 470 and 500 nm as their emission region lies between 400 and 550 nm. Different excitation wavelengths were chosen to check the presence of different fluorophores and Fig. 7a–d represents the lifetime measured at the three different excitation sources. At shorter wavelength excitation (370 nm; Fig. 7a), the average lifetime (τavg) of 1.12 ns was recorded for the blue emitters (λem = 470 nm). Moreover, τavg of 1.61 ns was observed for 370 nm excitation for the green emitters as seen in Fig. 7b. On the contrary, for 470 nm and 500 nm excitations (emission at 534 nm in both cases) as portrayed in Fig. 7c and d, the measured average lifetimes were 0.77 ns and 0.69 ns, respectively. This excitation wavelength-dependent lifetime values suggest that increasing excitation wavelength activates shorter lifetime contributors [47]. Hence, at shorter wavelength (370 nm), CDs exhibited longer fluorescence lifetime, surpassing the lifetime observed at longer wavelength (470 nm and 500 nm). The existence of several lifetimes could imply presence of different emissive states due to various chemical environments on the surface of CDs [47]. The decay curve of CDs was fitted by multi-exponential function [F(t) = A1 exp.(−t/τ1) + A2 exp.(−t/τ2) + A3 exp.(−t/τ3)]. The Supplementary Table (ST1) provides the different fitted lifetime values corresponding to different excitation source.

It is known that the CDs that possess numerous carboxyl and hydroxyl groups can be easily dispersed in polar solvents, but are immiscible in non-polar solvents. Hence, the effect of solvents on the optical features of the CDs was investigated. The CDs were soluble in polar protic solvents including water, methanol, ethanol, glycerol and isopropanol as well as in polar aprotic solvents such as DMF and DMSO. The UV spectra for the CDs dispersed in different solvents are presented in SF7a. The two transitions are represented by two peaks corresponding to π → π* and n → π* transitions. The SF7b & c depicts the photographs of CDs dispersed in different solvents under daylight and UV illumination, respectively. Further, the emission spectra were recorded to understand the contribution of solvation towards fluorescence. The solvent dependent shift in the fluorescence observed in polar protic solvents can be ascribed to Hydrogen bonding, while in case of polar aprotic solvents dipole-dipole interactions are predominant [48,49]. There was a profound hypsochromic shift in the blue emission (λem = 470 nm) in case of polar protic and aprotic solvents. On the contrary, there was no shift in the green emission (λem = 534 nm) as observed in SF7d.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formulation of security ink using CDs for flexography printing
ce:section.ce:para: Solvent-based inks release a lot of volatile organic compounds (VOC), which are harmful to the environment, and hence is one of the major concerns worldwide. Water-based inks do not emit VOCs that contribute to global warming, and are gaining popularity due to their environmental friendly characteristics. Previous research articles have documented the formulation of water-based flexo ink using organic molecules as fluorescent pigments [12]. In this study, a water-based fluorescent ink, which is a homogenous mixture of CDs distributed in deioinsed water, glycerol and additives including surfactant, defoamer, and wax (Table 1 ) is reported for the first time to the best of our knowledge. The pale yellow coloured flexo-ink formulation (SF8a) with pH of 7.5 displayed bluish green fluorescence under 365 nm UV irradiation as presented in SF8b.

The influence of the individual components of the ink: glycerol, surfactant, defoamer and wax on the absorbance and emission characteristics of CDs were also investigated. The respective UV spectra as portrayed in Fig. 8a displayed two unequal absorption curves corresponding to π → π* and n → π* transitions. The photographs of CDs mixed with the different components of ink under daylight and 365 nm UV illuminations are depicted in SF8c & d. The glycerol in which CDs were evenly scattered showed emission at 473 nm and a shoulder peak at 534 nm as presented in Fig. 8b. The addition of surfactant and defoamer to the CDs also showed a similar pattern. However, the addition of wax exhibited a 17 nm red shift from 473 nm to 490 nm. When the CDs were mixed with glycerol alone, the fluorescence intensity was not affected much, whereas when the CDs were distributed in surfactant or defoamer, the emission intensity decreased steadily, indicating their probable interaction with the surface groups of the CDs. Furthermore, the dispersion of CDs in wax dramatically reduced the fluorescence intensity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of flexo-coats/prints on UV dull paper substrates
ce:section.ce:para: According to the International Commission on Illumination, chromaticity parameters or colour changes of prints are monitored using 3D CIELAB colour spaces (designed to imitate human vision). CIE suggests the use of L*, a*, b* values to construct colour description. The L* value ranges from 0 to 100, closely mimicking human sense of image lightness/luminance and assisting in the prediction of small colour fluctuations. The L* value drops in case of yellowing. The two chromatic components that range from −120 to 120 are a* (from green specified by negative values to red specified by positive values) and b* (from blue specified by negative values to yellow specified by positive values). The print is invisible if the ΔE value is between 0 and 2, while specialists can recognise if the value range is 2–3, and common people can recognise if it is 3 or more. As per ISO 12647-6 standards, the specific colorimetric values of L* ≥90, a* ≤3, and b* ≤5 are assigned to the paper substrate for flexographic printing. In the present study, UV dull papers of thickness 0.121 mm with L*, a*, b* and density values of 91.79, −0.64, 4.85, and 0.13, respectively were used.

At room temperature, the print proofs dried up immediately. The coats (three layers) of the flexo ink obtained using draw-down technique and the flexo-printed (5 layers) solid patch of 105 mm × 297 mm dimension on UV-dull paper were undetectable in daylight but turned on a bluish-green fluorescence when exposed to 365 nm UV irradiation (SF9a & b). The emission spectra (SF9d) of the blank paper displayed a strong peak at 440 nm and a weak one at 531 nm. Interestingly, the coats and prints exhibited a blue emission at 450 nm and a red shifted yellow emission of the CDs at 571–574 nm, in addition to a small emission of the blank paper at 532 nm. Therefore, a yellow pass filter that cuts off the lower wavelength emissions was used to view the yellow fluorescence (emission at 574 nm) from the printed patch as presented in SF9c under 365 nm UV light. Further, the UV curing equipment was used to test the photostability of the ink film (single print) by exposing the printed samples (single layer) of 1.0 × 1.0 cm dimension to UV light at 300 W/30 m/min for 0, 10, 20, and 30 passes. The emission spectra of zero pass (unexposed to UV) prints were compared with the samples exposed to UV for 10, 20, and 30 passes (SF10). Interestingly, the emission intensity increased till 20 passes and further upon 30 passes decreased to almost similar intensity as, that of unexposed sample, which confirmed the good photostability of the ink. The colorimetric and densitometric parameters were recorded for the UV exposed and unexposed print proofs and are listed in ST2, and the variations of L*, a* and b* values are graphically represented in Fig. 9a. The value of L* for UV dull paper was 91.79, and did not deviate even after printing, which indicated that the colour viewed is white. For both unprinted and printed samples, a negative value of a* specified green colour, whereas a positive value of b implied yellow colour. The ΔE values of unexposed and UV exposed (10, 20 and 30 pass) print proofs were <1, and hence the prints appear invisible to human eye.

The degree to which the original print endures repeated rubbing and scratching is known as abrasion resistance. The purpose of the test is to mimic the kind and level of damage that can occur when the paper is brushed across a print. Printed and unprinted strips of 8 × 5 cm dimension were fixed to the base plate and the rubber pad, respectively. About 2 lbs. weight was placed on the samples with the test block rubber pads covering the ink in order to gauge their resilience to rubbing impacts. The colour parameters and optical density measurements recorded after 50 and 100 strokes are listed in ST3. The ΔE values for 50 and 100 rubs indicate that the print proofs appear invisible. The graphical representation of L*, a* and b values as depicted in Fig. 9b revealed that the print proofs have good abrasion resistance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anti-counterfeiting application of flexo-ink formulation with dual emissive CDs
Dual emissive ink for document authentication
ce:section.ce:para: To differentiate between genuine and forged products, security printing inks that offer distinct optical features and remarkable covertness are essential. Anti-counterfeiting patterns that are invisible in daylight are difficult to replicate, and can be printed on products such as fabric, banknotes, documents, and certificates. In the present report, CDs were used to formulate a flexo ink and print on security paper using flexography technique. Though the flexo ink displayed emission due to CDs at 490 and 534 nm, the coat/print exhibited good amount of covertness as they appeared colourless in daylight, and displayed a bluish-green fluorescence under 360 nm UV light illumination as well as a yellow emission under the same light source when a lower wavelength cut-off filter was used. The unique security property of the formulated ink was also analysed by cutting out ‘MAHE’ from a UV dull paper, and further painted with the flexo-ink formulation using a brush. The emission spectra of blank and flexo ink coated ‘MAHE’ cut-outs as presented in Fig. 9c clearly depicts the dual emissions (blue at 452 nm and yellow at 573 nm) of the prints obtained from CD-derived flexo ink. The Fig. 9d shows the image of the cut-out under daylight, whereas the bluish green fluorescence as presented in Fig. 9e was perceived when illuminated with 365 nm UV source. Moreover, as the CDs emitted in yellow region in addition to blue, a yellow long pass filter which effectively cuts off the blue emissions was used to view the yellow fluorescence from the ‘MAHE’ cut-outs under 365 nm UV illumination (Fig. 9f). Thus to the best of our knowledge this is the first comprehensive report on dual emissive CDs based flexo ink and its coating/print which cannot be easily forged: the forger can view only the blue emission upon UV-illumination, whereas the user can check the authenticity of the print by observing the yellow fluorescence using a long-pass filter which can effectively cut-off shorter wavelength emissions. Thus the eco-friendly flexo-ink formulated from natural precursor-derived dual emissive CDs isolated through column chromatography technique can deliver superior security features to the prints: i) invisible under daylight, as well as ii) intense bluish-green and iii) yellow emissions while using a yellow long pass filter upon 365 nm illumination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Flexo ink from CDs for flexible electronics in anti-counterfeiting
ce:section.ce:para: Flexible electronics that are cost-effective, recyclable, and lightweight for commercial anti-counterfeiting applications, especially those printed on paper substrates using eco-friendly inks can provide several benefits including quick and simple mass production with minimal waste. Moreover, high priority has been given to the development of next-generation printed flexible electronics for the Internet of Things (IoT), RFID-based technologies, organic thin-film transistors (OTFTs), and passive/active electronics [50–52]. In this context, surface roughness and electrical conductivity of the prints are important for any electronic device [53].

The surface topographies of the unprinted paper and print proofs were analysed using the AFM images (SF11). In comparison to unprinted paper, the average surface roughness (Ra: 441.3 for unprinted & 261.8 nm for print proofs), root mean square roughness (Rq: 591.6 & 362.4 nm), and maximum roughness depth values (Rmax: 2.018 μm &1.265 μm) indicated a uniform and smooth ink layer filling the porous and fibrous paper substrate in the print proofs. To further reduce surface roughness and improve device performance, smooth surface coating or appropriately developed paper with minimal innate surface roughness can be used.

Hall effect measurements can shed light on a functional material's inherent charge-transport properties and mobility for its application in electronic devices. As the CDs synthesized from Tapioca as the natural precursor showed graphitic and polycrystalline nature, the electrical characteristics of the flexo ink prints were examined. The electrical parameters such as Hall coefficient of 29.4847 cm3/C, mobility of 5.4668 cm2/Vs, carrier density of 2.11 × 1017 cm−3, electrical conductivity of 18.5412 Ω−1 m−1, resistivity of 0.053933 Ωm and bulk conductivity of 37.0825 Ω−1 m−1 were obtained for the print proof. These results indicated the suitability of the water-based CD-derived eco-friendly flexo ink formulation as active channel layer for OTFT device, which could be used on bank notes or security papers for anti-counterfeiting applications [54]. The CD-based flexo ink composition can also be explored to construct an adhesive flexible antenna with low resistance for RFID tags using flexography, which is a low-cost solution processing method.

In the present study, CDs were prepared using a mild hydrothermal process from the inner starch-rich flesh of tapioca roots of cassava plant as a green precursor. The purification and isolation of the CDs employed a simple fractionation method involving a silica column and an elution system comprising of DCM/methanol. UV–Vis, ATR-IR, and photoluminescence spectral characterization were used to study the different fractions obtained through column chromatography. The fraction with desirable emission features was chosen for further studies including characterization, water-based security ink formulation, printing using flexography, and anti-counterfeiting application. The graphitic nature of the CDs was indicated in the XRD and RS data. XPS, EDS and SAED proved the presence of specific elements and functional groups. TCSPC measurement demonstrated existence of several lifetimes that could imply presence of different emissive states due to various chemical environments on the surface of CDs. The zeta potential value indicated good colloidal stability of the synthesized CDs. Moreover, the TEM imaging suggested the formation of spherical CDs, which were evenly dispersed without any aggregation. A water-based, eco-friendly security ink with the CDs as fluorophores was formulated and printed on UV-dull paper using flexography. The invisible prints displayed bluish-green fluorescence upon a 365 nm illumination and also demonstrated good colour stability, high adhesion, and abrasion resistance. The multi-level security features of the flexo-ink was validated by observing the invisible letters that exhibited an intense bluish-green emission upon illuminating with a 365 nm light source, and a yellow emission when viewed using a yellow long pass filter under the same UV light excitation source. Besides, the smooth surface and attractive electrical parameters of the flexo-prints suggested the probable use of the ink in flexible printed electronics for security purposes. This comprehensive study thus reports the synthesis of green CDs, their purification and characterization, formulation of an eco-friendly water-based flexo ink with dual emission and investigation of print properties for anti-counterfeiting applications.
Supplementary data provided. The datasets generated during and/or analysed during the current study are available from the first author on reasonable request.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge Central Research Facility (CRF), NITK, Central Instrumentation Facility (CIF), IISER Thiruvananthapuram, Sophisticated Analytical Instrument Facility (SAIF), IIT-Bombay and Central Research Facility (CRF) at Centre for Nano and Soft Matter Sciences for providing analytical facility for the research work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.porgcoat.2022.107233.

[2] P. Aldhous, Counterfeit pharmaceuticals: murder by medicine, Nature (2005) 434.
[3] G. Hess, Counterfeit pesticides damage EU economy, C&EN Global Enterprise. 95 (2017) 16.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: On the molecular origin of photoluminescence of nonblinking carbon dot
